food_subgroup: Cooked vegetable dish in spiced sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cooked broccoli food product entry; no specific broccoli masala entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a broccoli masala composite dish code. A00LC (Broccoli, cooked) used as the primary ingredient anchor. Masala sauce components are secondary.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for broccoli masala as a composite dish. Not fabricated.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Broccoli Masala is approximately 200 g, representing one side dish or vegetable curry portion as served in North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11091); supplemented with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for masala sauce component estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact broccoli masala composite entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked broccoli (NDB 11091) as the primary ingredient (~65% by weight) with a standard North Indian masala sauce component (onion, tomato, oil, spices, ~35% by weight) derived from ICMR-NIN 2017 vegetable curry sauce reference values. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate recalculated proportionally. Sodium elevated to reflect added salt and spice blend typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database reference for non-starchy cooked vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for broccoli masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on the analogous entry for cooked non-starchy vegetables (broccoli, cauliflower, mixed vegetable curry) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Broccoli is a low-GI non-starchy vegetable; the masala sauce adds minimal digestible carbohydrate. GL per 200 g serving calculated as (15 × 6.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.95, rounded to 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blend in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: Restaurant-prepared Broccoli Masala contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (620 mg per typical 200 g serving), which represents about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from broccoli
type2_diabetes.guidance: Broccoli Masala has a low estimated GI of 15 and provides 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting glycaemic control; the low glycaemic load of approximately 2.0 per 200 g serving makes it a suitable vegetable dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high fibre, and presence of cardioprotective phytonutrients in broccoli
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Broccoli Masala is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and cholesterol-free, and broccoli provides sulforaphane and folate with established cardioprotective associations; the primary cardiovascular concern is the moderate sodium content in restaurant preparations, which should be managed by requesting lower-salt cooking.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Broccoli Masala is a North Indian vegetable curry dish consisting of broccoli florets cooked in a mildly spiced golden sauce made from onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. The dish is typically prepared by sautéing aromatics in oil until golden, adding tomato and spice paste, then simmering broccoli florets until tender but retaining some bite. The result is a vibrant, mildly spicy dish with earthy, slightly bitter broccoli balanced by the tangy sweetness of the tomato-onion base. In Singapore, Broccoli Masala is served as a vegetable side dish in North Indian restaurants, often accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI dish providing approximately 72 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful dietary fibre (2.4 g), vitamin C (38 mg), folate (55 mcg), and iron (0.9 mg). It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The low glycaemic index and high fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate at restaurant level and should be monitored by hypertensive individuals. The dish is a nutritionally positive choice within an Indian restaurant meal context.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Broccoli Masala?
Is Broccoli Masala good for diabetics?
What spices are in Broccoli Masala?
Is Broccoli Masala high in sodium?
What is the GI of Broccoli Masala?
Where can I find Broccoli Masala in Singapore?
meal_context: Vegetable curry accompaniment to naan or roti
Part of a shared Indian meal
Vegetarian main or side in a thali-style meal
Suitable for diabetic-friendly Indian meal planning

confidence_reason: No exact broccoli masala composite dish entry exists in ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked broccoli data with ICMR-NIN 2017 vegetable curry sauce reference values in proportional weighting. GI was estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy cooked vegetable references. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (broccoli) has strong approved-source data and the masala sauce component is well-characterised in ICMR-NIN.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level approved sources (USDA SR Legacy NDB 11091 for cooked broccoli; ICMR-NIN 2017 for masala sauce component). No single composite entry for broccoli masala was found in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are reported as 0.0 because this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; these are naturally zero for a vegan vegetable curry. 3. Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish with no animal fat; naturally zero. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero by ingredient profile. 5. Sodium of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home-prepared versions would be lower. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for broccoli masala has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 7. Price range SGD 14.90–17.90 preserved from field survey data for reference only; not used as a nutritional evidence source. 8. Eight restaurants on field survey data serve this dish as of the seed record date.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked broccoli data with ICMR-NIN 2017 masala sauce component reference values.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Indian cuisine in Singapore context.
Estimated GI at 15 by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy cooked vegetable references; documented estimation basis fully.
Added zero-macro justification notes for vitamin B12, vitamin D, cholesterol, and trans fat (all naturally zero for a vegan plant-based dish).
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency before finalising.
Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'close' to 'closest_parent' to comply with G5 ontology honesty hard gate requirements.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
